ZURRENT AFFAIRS,

With mingled feeling of pleasure and regret, we are
sure, nearly all our readers received the news that our
distinguished contributor, “M.O'R.,”” had been ap-
pointed to the position of Rector of the Irish College
at Rome. We say nearly all our readers for we are
sure that some would be glad to hear that the brilliant
Doctor had been sent to Timbuctoo; he is a sort of man

* who is hated as well as loved, and as many of the As-
cendancy cannot resist the temptation to have a weekly
peep at the LEADER, it numbers amongst its redders a
small minority who have no kindly feelings for Dr.
O’Riordan. Though going to. Rome he will not be lost
to Ireland, and we hope occasionally to have “ M.O'R."”
at the end of many interesting articles in our columns.

We take the following from the Independent of Sep-
tember 1st;:—* The Rev. Dr. O’Riordan, who has just
* been appointed rector of the Irish College at Rome, is
best known as the man whose relentless logic swept away
half-a-million of Sir Horace Plunkett’s misconceptions
to Irish Catholicism. But, apart from his valuable

contributions to the literature of public matters, he has
a high reputation for original research. His pamphlet
on “The Popes as Peacemakers’’ (published by the
Catholic Truth Society) is a marvel of unfamiliar learn-
ing, presented with rare brevity and interest. In liter-
ary style he is dispassionate, relying on well marshalled
arguments, and carefully shunning ‘mere eloguence.’
He always convinces, and he never persuades. It is
to be hoped that his departure from Ireland will not
rob Irish journalism of what it can so ill-afford—the
respected 1nitials, ‘M. O'R."”

The temperance van, as a means of promoting tem-
perance and rational refreshments at fairs and meet-
ings, has not been given any considerable trial in Ire-
land yet. If properly worked, one would expect that
it should be capable of doing a vast amount of practical
temperance work. A priest writes to us that he started
a van to attend local fairs and meetings, but that his
success has not been quite satisfactory. Those whose
attention he wished to engage, the young men and
boys, as a rule, avoided the van, and patronised the
tent or public-house. . Our correspondent would like
some practical advice. “ Could you, sir, offer any sug-
gestion through the medium of your paper, or pri-
vately, that might forward the interests we all have so
much at heart?’ There must be several of our
readers who have experience of working temperance
vans, and, perhaps, they would give our correspondent
and the public generally the benefit of their advice on
this practical and very important department of the
temperance movement.

We know of the attempts of the British Tobacco
Combine to dump “ Irish Roll ”’ made in England upon
the Irish market. We have before us a type-written
letter from a British tobacco and cigarette firm which
shows how eager they are to increase their hold on the
Irish market. The Leaper has hit a good many one-
time British dumpers in Ireland; we are sure the tra-
vellers for British and Scotch cloths in the Irish mar-
ket have a decidedly sanguinary regard for us. ~ Well,
to return to the tobacco. A London firm with a
foreign name are proprietors of a certain line of tobacco
and cigarettes; the line is called by a name with a dis-
tinct and unmistakable Irish flavour. They are anxi-
ous to secure the custom of the medical men of Ireland.
This Cockney tobacco firm would like to use the doctors
of Ireland as touts for their goods, and if any Irish
doctors would act as touts for a smart Cockney tobacco
firm, we would not think much of their sense of dignity.
Indeed, the suggestion of the Cockney firm is nothing
short of an insult to the medical profession of Ireland.
Every brainy doctor in Ireland reads the LeapEr—if
there are any exceptions they ought to be spoken to by
their friends for their own good—and these men are
not going to turn themselves into tobacco touts for a
Cockney firm. This firm profess to offer cigarettes at
a reduced price to doctors. In the course of the tout-
ing letter, we read:—“I, perhaps, ought to explain
that although I ask you to treat the very low quota-
tions given as confidential, I do not ask you to, in any
way, act as an advertising agent on my behalf, but
simply suggest—that in the évent of the . . cigars,
cigarettes or smoking mixture giving you absolute
satisfaction—that you will, as occasion arises, recommend.
the same to your friends in the same manner that you
would recommend any other article tHat.you had every
confidence in.””  We trust that all our readers of the
medical profession will recommend, if they have occa-
sion to make any reference to the matter, that their
friends should stand by the Irish tobacco manufac-
turers who are making such a plucky fight against the
Combine and other British firms.

Movement for his impressions of the Connacht Feis,
and in reply he writes:— The usual wealth of Inish
was in evidence. Irish seemed to be in the very air. The
competitors, the adjudicators, the stewards, and even
the onlookers spoke only Irish. In Galway ome is
forced to realise that Irish is a living language, that it
needs no revival, but give it an opportunity of assert-
ing itself. In other provinces we have Irish writers,
but in Connacht, especially in Galway, we have Irish
speakers. It is regrettable that the many stories, songs,
ete., heard at the peip Connact, and the many others
to be found everywhere amongst the people, are mnot
brought to light. The only painful aspect of the Feip
was the great indifference of the Galway people. Many
could be seen from the North and South, and from all
parts of Ireland, but scarcely one from the town of
Galway even as onlookers. As for competitors from
the town, I don’t think I noticed one. Some effort
should be made to arouse interest in Feip Connacg,
and draw competitors from the whole province, and
make it what the Peip na Mumain is—representative of
the whole province.”’

The inequity of the Dublin civic contribution to the
D.M.P. is so absolutely glaring that even the ““ Irisk
Times that gloated over the proposed reduction by 22
of the Irish Parliamentary representation has to admit
it.  The [ibeller of Canon M‘Inerney, in the course of
a leading article, says:—“ We confess that we have a
great deal of sympathy, not with the Corporation,
which merely uses the ‘grievance’ for the purpose of
having a fling at the authorities, but with the rate-
payers of Dublin, in the matter of the police tax.”
And again:—“Tt does seem ridiculous that a force
which fifty years ago could be made efficient upon an
cightpenny rate now for no reason that we have been
able to ascertain requires three times that amount.
This is the thing which must be hammered into the
heads of the ratepayers; to quote statistics and com-
parative tables showing how much more expensive is
the Dublin Metropolitan Police than the police of Eng-
lish cities of the same size is little to the purpose just
now. What has to be done is to secure the admission
that the ratepayers of Dublin have a grievance.”” The
obvious comment on all this is that when the libeller of
Canon M‘Inerney admits the reality of an Irish griev-
ance, it must, indeed, be quite a thumping grievance.

As Alderman Reigh pointed out, the present tax of
£28,000 a year would, in the event of a revaluation of
the city, increase the police tax by about £7,000 a year
more; and Mr. Dinnage, a Unionist, declared that 1t
“was a shame and a great hardship that the citizens of
Dublin should be paying double for the police more

than was paid in Sheflield, Leeds, Manchester, and -

Liverpool.”” The monetary grievance is bad enough,
but the great grievance is that the citizens who pay all
this money have not, through the Corporation, one
atom of authority over the city police force. By a
majority of 29 votes to 4 it was decided not to pay the
police tax. It is now the police Commissioner’s turn
to move, and the development of the situation is a mat-
der of national interest.

Mr. Bergin of Nenagh, has been on tour through the
County of Limerick. He visited Birdhill, Castleconnell,
Patrick’s Well, Croom, Brura, Bruff, and Newport,
but was unable to purchase a bottle of Irish manufac-
tured hop bitters until he came within five miles of the
town of Nenagh. “ All the respectable traders’’ whom
Mr. Bergin called upon “ would be delighted to en-
courage Irish manufacture but they couldn’t get the
anti-Trish wholesale bottlers of Limerick to supply them
with the like.”” 1In this and other directions the whole-
sale houses in Ireland require to be rigorously dealt
with. We do not think that the forthcoming industrial
conference at Cork could discuss a more vital and im-
portant matter than the attitude of the wholesale houses
towards the Industrial Revival. Many of the retailers
are so heavily in debt to the wholesale houses that the
former probably do not feel themselves in a position to
press their views on the latter, and so are content to
take, to a great extent, whatever the wholesale houses
may send them. It is plain that the wholesale houses
have great power for good or evil on the fortunes of the
Industrial Revival, and the importance of bringing in-
fluence, or if needs be, pressure to bear upon them is
manifest. The forthcoming Conference at Cork should
be able to devise some means of effectively tackling the
wholesale house difficulfy.

Mr. Bergin noticed whilst passing the principal
Street of Limerick, that foreign biscuits were exhibited
for sale in some of the largest wholesale grocers’ win-
dows. Glasgow sweets and foreign chocolate were also
in evidence. He noticed in several drapers’ windows
an exhortation to wear Irvish hats, and on calling into
some of the places the first hat he was havded was
stamped “ Superior, London manufacture.’”’

Tho Bishop of Raphoe is to preach a charity sermon
at Glenties on the first Sunday in October. The object
of the appeal is to help to clear off a debt of some
£2,600 that remains on the new Parochial House,
Glenties, and on the church in process of erection for
the accommodation of the people of Croaghs.  The
Parish Priest of Glenties is Canon M‘Fadden, and
Canon M‘Fadden, of Glentics, is the Father M‘Fadden
whose name is linked in rather recent history with the
struggles at Gweodore. The new church will stand in
the wilds of the Donegal Iighlands, and will accommo-
date some families who live as far as fourtecen miles
from the present parvish church. As his lordship, Dr.
O’Donnell, points out in a letter to Canon M‘Fadden
the peoplo for whom the church is being built, hhoug‘ﬁ
suro to subscribo with traditional generosity, are too
poor to discharge the whole debt that the church will
croate. We think it is a case that will appeal to
generous Irish hearts in all parts of the world, and we
havo pleasure in specially directing the attention of our

An earnest Irish Irelander, \;vhose opinion on the

- merits of an Irish school is well worth having, has

drawn our attention to an omission occurring in our re-
marks recently on Irish boarding schools. This week
we gladly supply the omission which is concerned with
the. Ursuline Convent, Blackrock, Cork. ‘We have
touched on the Blackrock Convent before. Our last
re'ference to it, if we remember rightly, in connection
with the very successful production of an Irish play by
the pupils at the Convent. The play was Aov 6 Neill
by Conan Muaot.

_As we have said, the list of Irish educational estab-
lishments is so big that there should be no excuse for
parents sending the future manhood and womanhood
of Ireland to English schools. Even in the Intellectual

Sahara there are oases such as St. Malachy’s College,
Belfast.

Did you ever hear of Mooney’s —— ink? The
Mooney’s are a particularly versatile class. To most
people, though not of course to the readers of this paper,
the name suggests the glass that cheers and also, alas,
inebriates; yet one of the hardest working temperance
advocates is a man of that name too, Mr. Joseph
Mooney. However, we are concerned now with Messrs.
Mooney of Dundalk, manufacturers of ink,—spectrum
ink. They have sent us on several samples of their
inks, and we have already tested their ordinary writing
fluid. Messrs. Mooney, of Dundalk, have no mis-
givings themselves as to the quality of their writing ink
for they sent us a bottle of a well-known British ink—
largely used in Ireland, so that we might be able to
compare the Irish manufactured ink with the British
stuff. Now, we have been writing with both inks dur-
ing the past few days, and our honest judgment is that
the Irish ink is the better of the two. The first im-
pression of the British ink is weak in comparison with
the first impression of the Irish ink; in due course both
inks turn black, but the Irish ink retains its superiority
so far as the ordinary eye can judge. The Irish bill for
imported ink must come to a considerable sum in the
course of a year, and it is pleasant to find a new Irish
firm competing in the market. Every user of British
ink in Ireland ought in common fairness to Irish in-
dustry, give this Dundalk made ink a trial and con-
tinue to give it support if, in their judgment, they find
it satisfactory.

The current number of the New Ireland Review con-
tains several interesting articles. Mr. G. O’Donovan
writes on the ‘Latest Report on Irish Intermediate
education’’; Father Barry, C.SS.R., contributed a
fine article on an old but ever interesting personality,
James Clarence Mangan ; Mr. Arthur Clery, K.C., con-
tinues his studies in old Irish history, and Mr. Wil-
liam Dawson deals in a critical and sympathetic man-
ner with “ M.O’R.’s”’ book, “ Catholicity and Progress
in Ireland.”” The first paragraph of a review of “The
Philosophy of Irish Ireland,’”” runs as follows:— There
are questions of philosophy underlying the changes
through which the history of a nation unfolds itself.
We are accustomed to the phrase ‘the philosophy of
history ” and its meaning, so far as it has been defined by
those who use it, seems to be the principles of social life
which control the growth, or bring about the extinction
of nations. Tt would be strange if a history so eventful
as that of Ireland did not furnish a striking example of
the application of these principles. The latest ox-
ponent of these principles, as applied to Ireland, is Mr.
D. P. Moran, who, in a vigorous and trenchant style,
with which we are all familiar, states his views on this
question, which to us.is of such vital importance, Theso
views will not be novel to the readers of the New Ire-
land Review. Mr, Moran’s book consists of a series of
articles published in this review in the years 1898-1900.
He was not then such a well-known publicist as he is
now. But even then the boldness and courage of con-
viction which he exhibited, and the fresh and bracing
style in which® he oxpressed his opinions, attracted.
goneral attention.  These characteristics, apart from

There is a paper called the Fermanagh Times. It is
a “true blue ”’ paper. It will be remembered that last
year the cs.\:ztltcc{g position of midwife for the Florence-
court Dispensary District was vacant, and a secret
whip signed by five persons, one of them my Lord
Corry, was senf out, and in the course of this whip it
was writton :— We hear that there will be a Protes-
tant candidate for the post, and if every Unionist Poor
Law Guardian attends, she should have a good chance
of being clected. Therefore, on behalf of the Protestant
inhabitants of the Florencecourt Dispensary District, we
carnestly trust that you will do your utmost to at-
tend.”” " This circular, and the proceedings in the ad-
joining County of Tyrone, in which the man Coote,
Justice of the Peace, played such a prominent part,
are naturally called to mind by the article in the Fer-
managh Ttmes of August 3lst.  The article is called
“The Duty of Ulster.””  The articles commences:—
“The time is approaching rapidly when the Protestant
people of Ulster must take serious notice of the man-
ner in which all public appointments are being filled in
the other provinces.”” In “ other provinces’’ is good;
might we suggest that the time has come when the Pro-
testant people of Ulster might look to the manner in
which my Lord Corry and his friends behave themselves
in relation to even such an obscure job as the position
of midwife for a local Ulster dispensary district? We
are treated to a diatribe on the “intolerance’’ of the
local Boards in the South and West, and then we are
told : —“ If Protestants are to be excluded from the
public and progressive life of the other provinces they
can, at least, take care of their own interests in the
North.  The Protestants of Ulster are bound to see
that if their co-religionists cannot get employment in
other parts of the country they will, at least, get all
that is going amongst them. This is a matter that re-
quires plain speaking.””  There is much unconscious
humour in all this. Picture an attempt to goad, say,
the Belfast Corporation, out of its ““tolerant’’ atti-
tude towards “ Idolators.”

~ The arrangements for the Industrial Conference at
Cork are being pushed forward. The Conference will
be opened by the Lord Mayor of Cork, and it will be
presided .over by Dr. C. A, Windle, President of the
Queen’s College, Cork. About 200 delegates from all
parts of Ireland are expected. Several interesting mat-
ters are down for discussion, and upon these, or some
of them, we will probably have some remarks to make
between this and the assembling of the Conference.

We hope, any seeming legal difficulties to the con-
trary notwithstanding, that the Dublin Corporation will
do its utmost to assert its control over the class of pos-
ters that not infrequently disgrace the hoardings of
Dublin. The Freeman, an advertiser of “Zaza’’ and
“XKitty Grey,”’ cannot be called squeamish in these
matters, and yet in a note that paper says:— The mat-
ter is of such importance as to warrant fuller considera-
tion. It would seem to most people that some, at
least, of those objectionable posters come well within
the description of the prohibitory Act.  But, apart

~ from legal prohibition; surely public opinion could make

itself felt.”” Well, notwithstanding the public opinion
that we voiced concerning such things as “ Zaza’’ and
“Kitty Grey,” the Freeman inserted their advertise-
_ments, and we fear would do so again if these atrocities
re-visited Dublin. Public opinion without machinery
to make its force effective is not always a very powerful
thing. We hope some members of the Corporation will
bring this matter of obscene posters up again.

Already it would appear that if there is not exactly
a “split”’ in the Devolution camp, Mr. O’Brien’s
“revived ”’ Irish People will not be allowed to be the
only new weekly organ of ‘conciliation.”” There is
another weekly organ looming on the horizon. Woe
gather from a provincial contemporary that a weekly
paper in the T. W. Russellite interest is soon to make
its appearance in Belfast. The new Russellite paper,
the name of which has not yet transpired, is expected
to appear in the middle of this month. T. W. and
William are both personalities, and those who know
anything of human nature know that a personality is
a personality, and they may not unreasonably expect
crackers. What does T. W. think of William, and with
what adjectives will William qualify T.W.% Which of
them will first challenge the other to prove that his
paper has twice the circulation of the other fellow’s.

CORRESPONDENCE.

Irise, HAT MANUFACTURERS PLEASE NOTE.

My Dear Sir,—Having been informed that THE
LEADER is the chief exponent in Ireland of the Irish
Industrial Movement, I take the liberty of writing for
information concerning the manufacturers of hats. I
am in the hat business in Chicago and have thought it
might be possible to dispose of hats of Irish manufacture
in this city. While circumstances are such that it
would be necessary to handle these goods on a purely
business basis, still my sentiments are that I should
like to have goods not only made by Irishmen in Ire-
land, but that those who would sell me the goods be
of the same nationality.

I understand that many of the industries carried on
in Ireland are under control of people from the adjoin-
ing country. I would request that you give me the
names of one or more manufacturers who answer the
above requirements.

The city of Cork would be preferable as to location
as the carrying charges would be less and the transpor-
tation facilities more direct from that point. Wishing
success to the Irish Industrial Movement,—I beg to re-
main sincerely, ««MicHAEL J. HANLEY.

127 Dearborn Street,

Chicago, Ill., U.S.A.

CONCERNING SENTIMENT.

ENTIMENT is the greatest force in life.  Every
man possesses it and every nation also—England
by no means excluded. No sensible man denies the
force of sentiment; but what able men are constantly
attempting to do is to harness sentiment to the views
and movements which they desire to go ahead. No
one denies the force of the Shannon, that great
force, like the force of non-directed sentiment, that
flows idly to the sea. The problem for able men who
recognise the force of sentiment as they recognise the
force of the Shannon’s water flow is to hitch on such
force to some machinery or other, so that instead of, as
in the case of the Shannon, the force running idly to
the sea, and perhaps at times inundating corn lands, it
may turn something that ministers to some useful pur-
ose.
2 Sentiment, like many other things, is apt to get
mixed up in the mental fog of the general mind. There
are people who would almost make a corner in sentiment,
the class of people are usually those who consider that
sentiment in its raw, very raw, and uncontrolled, state
is everything ; many others on the other hand who sneer
at sentiment have a very lively appreciation of its force,
but owing to confusion of thought they might, were
such a classification requested of them, place themselves
on record in the census paper as anti-sentimentalists—
what they would really mean would be that the blowing
off of sentiment to the heavens as the Shannon flows
idly to the sea is of no value, rather a non-value in their
view. :

Of course we all admit the reality and force of senti-
ment, the great world compeller. What many of us
are anxious for is that it be utilized to work as much
beneficent machinery as possible. We all have a senti-
ment in favour of Irish industry, for instance; but the
practical problem is to utilise that sentiment through
such machinery as is calculated to drive industries
ahoad, just as the force of steam in an engine of the
G. 8. and W. Railway is so directed that you are
taken at so many miles an hour from Dublin to Cork.
Supposing that the G- 8. and W. Railway people erected
an open boiler at Kingsbridge and placed a steam en-
gino ten yards away, attachod to which were several
carriages full of passengers desirous of getting to Cork ;
supposing that Napoleon Dent, the imported *mana-
ger,” lit a great fire under this boiler. The water in
the open boiler would in due time be converted into
steam ; hours would pass by and the steam from the
open boiler would ascend in volumes skywards clouding
the view; at length some impatient passenger would
look out and ask why the train was not moving. = Some
sonsible person would point out that in order to estab-
lish motion the steam should be generated in the boiler
of the engine which, had ingenious mechanism attached.
Thon what we may call a green steamist would arise
and denounce that sensible man as a non-believer in
steam. He would tell him that steam was a great.

|

|

power in the mechanical®world, and that he should sit

“down and' not make a fool of himself;; did he not see
“ the steam rising in volumes from the open boiler, and
“ what more did he want?

There you have a parallel to our green sentimentalists.
‘We who believe in the force of sentiment as much as
- any other people, see it going off in volumes from an
open boiler into the air. If we complain that we are not
.moving, that undirected and unharnessed sentiment
-going up into the sky will carry us nowhere, we are
-probably cynics, forsooth—or more dreadful still, ma-
terialists—perhaps, even, scoffers! All the while we
believe in sentiment as one of the great forces in the
human world; what we want is that instead of being
generated in an open boiler where it will run to waste,
probably in running make a cloud that will do damage,
we consider that sentiment should be generated in
properly formed boilers connected with mechanism
which would insure that the sentimental force turned
things. If you object to generating sentiment in an
open boiler and ask for machinery you will probably
be told that you have no—ideals!” Even yet we are a
green people.

THE OBSTACLES OF INDUSTRIAL ENTERPRISE.

T is a common idea that want of adequate capital is
l a great obstacle to industrial development in Ire-
iand.  Unlike too many commeon ideas, it is a true
one. If “capital’”’ means money available for indus-
trial enterprise, then Ireland is not a wealthy country,
but a very poor one. If, however, capital merely
means saved or accreted wealth, Ireland is not so very
poor as one might think. According to official figures
we are supposed to be worth something over ninety mil-
lions sterling in cash and Government Securities. This
sum was made up as follows in 1903 :—

This ninety millions, as will be seen, does not include
the money invested in Railways, Tramways, Municipal
Stocks, and various other sound securities. However,
taking the sum of ninety millions by itself, one would
suppose that it afforded “ample scope and verge
enough ”’ for a fairly liberal amount of industrial
enterprise—at all events, for something more in that
way than we see going forward at present. This, how-
ever, is an illusion; it proves so when we look how the
ninety millions are made up. To hegin with, nearly
twelve millions of the ninety belong to small holders,
for such I count the Savings Bank depositors; the
exact figures are £11,830,000. Take this from your
ninety millions, and you have a trifle over seventy-
eight millions left. Of this sum far more than half
consists of money held by the Banks (Joint Stock), and,
of course, the extent of the average holding of the
depositors i3 a sacred mystery. It is more than prob-
able, however, that there are many holders in the Say-

whose holding is greater than the net available balance
of many a petty trader, who, for reasons of business

haye their money employed “in their trade, and who

ON TOUR IN THE DECIES.—III

¥ O sum up, and generally, I find this district is bi-

lingual,—when it likes. That business is not
<one in Gaelic except in Ring peninsula. That in
another generation Gaelic will be to seek in said Ring—
that is if Governmenf doesn’t make the teaching of it
in the schools there compulsory. That the people
generally are ripe to be taught Esperanto—anything for
a change. That the Gaelic League tcachers are called
“the Irishmen.”” Finally, tlrat Waterford City is still
“ Intacta.’”

As regards education, I understand that the bishop
has made a rule that by next year every teacher in the
diocese must have a certificate to teach Gaelic. Also
that in every congregation containing Gaelic speakers a
sermon in Gaelic must be preached once a month hence-
forward. Which will be a revolution.

As to agriculture, I learn that labourers of all sorts
are scare and not worth the trouble it takes to humour
them. That’s the farmer’s side of the story. The
labourer’s side is that “ the farmers had their day, and
now, !?? Which perhaps marks another revolu-
tion. The willy-wagtail has, I learn, disappeared from
the Decies but crows are as plenty as ever. The turnip
fields in places give evidence of the latter fact. The
potatoes have been sprayed very generally; saw one
farmer with a spoutless teapot in the middle of a big
field spraying.  The Agricultural Department’s lec-
turers are not, I understand much listened to. Not
enough capital in country to carry out their directions.
Farmers, besides, rather old to go to school. Would cost
less perhaps and pay, better to teach the children? Har-
vest prospects fair. TFarmers going in for binder-and-
reaper on partnership system. Not working too well
however, as one man’s harvest can’t be got to wait till
his partner’s will have been cut. Solution of difficulty,
very big farms or very cheap machines.

Industrially T heard no great hum. Two breweries
in Dungarvan making own beer and bottling Guinness’s.
Also a new and going-ahead cider factory.  Some
tobacco made up in town. Good and growing woollen
factory close by, another, rather sleepy, at Kilmac-
‘thomas, a third near Kilmeaden. Shirts, bottles, beer,
boots, bacon, made in Waterford. TFishing, not much,
around coast. Irish manufacture fillipped into fashion
somewhat latterly in Dungarvan. Exception made in
case of one article only—the Gaelic language.
‘Creameries pretty general but giving way to private
separator system. Query, must those creamories be
‘kept horribly inartistic looking and dirty outside? Look
as though they did not think they had come to stay.
For the rest, some 50 per cent. of male population be-

‘tween 20 and 30 live by betting.

Socially the Decies is a pleasant place for a stranger.
He is looked up to (unless he happens to be from Tip-
perary—in Dungarvan that is); is, when he becomes
_known, pulled into public houses and treated unless he

Jkeops in middle of street; is not addressed in JGaehc; is
doforred. to in his every remark with a ¢ that’s thrue;’
is made feel at home in fact. Strange, native Recuhan-
ties of dress, etc., there are, too—the hats which have
latterly replaced the old hooded cloaks of the females
and which the latter are visibly afraid will momentarily
fall off; the “fine, aisy ’’ tallk of the men; the zv.‘!'fccted
carolossness, slattern bearing, high-pitched, tin-can
voices and blue-streaked visages of the gentry; and the
overything-but-the-tennis-racket get up of the rising
genoration of comfortable shop-keepers, etc.

Politically, the older generation are tired out, the
younger in the towns read Mr. O’Brien’s controversy
instead of Tit-Bits. Land Bill working very slowly.

company that other planned one, the denaturing of the

THE GENERAL COUNCIL.

T HE meetings of the General Council of County

| Councils have an interest for the student of Irish
politics very much greater than is warranted by the in-
dividual items of their proceedings. For he looks not
so much to the assembly of resolutions, trivial or plati-
tudinous or the consideration of the tiresome useful
topics of routine, as to the hopes which these discus-
sions afford of the birth of a new Irish institution. The
General Council is in truth a body with a problemati-
cal future, and at the present moment it is equally
likely that it will sink into declaratory nothingn or
that on the other hand it will become the recognised
organ for the expression of Irish opinion, and perhaps
also an efficient instrument for putting it into practice.
With the decision to discuss politics and the consequent
secession of East Ulster, this body entered on a new
career; it ceased to be parochial and abandoned that
shaded path which leads the leisured to the village
pump. How far it will be capable of advancing along
the broad high-road of national freedom is the question
that is still to be decided.

Among the many vagaries, political and constitu-
tional, to which the study of Magyar history gave birth
during the past year, there was to be found one fruitful
idea, that was the strengthening of the national position
by the careful organisation of existing institutions, the
policy of émperium in imperio, a method more practised
than adverted to in the days of Parnell. The great
difficulty in the way of such a policy has, however,
hitherto been the extreme weakness and insufficiency of
our institutions. What, for example, could be done
with the Board of National Education? The County
Council system has of course, as has been often pointed
out, placed new weapons in Irish hands. But weapons
are of themselves of little avail, and it will be upon the
oxtent to which these Councils are capable of being or-
ganised by such a body as the General Council that suc-
cess of any such policy must depend.

Now it is very apparent that the General Council is
as yebt far from sure of itself. A Parish Council, be-
come a Parliament, it fecls uneasy in its new dignity,
and is uncertain both of its powers and its limitations.
Its members are not yet sure how far they can control
and bring into line the conduct of the constituent bodies
whom they represent, nor how far powers given for
county may be utilised for national purposes. WWill
this body, powerless in itself, be able to make effective
use of its powerful constituonts and take a place in Ire-
land, liko that of the convention of Zemstvos in
Russia, The voice of Ireland has hitherto been ex-
pressed by national conventions of somewhat fortuitous
composition ; it remains to be seen whether this scienti-
fically representafive assemblage will be able to fulfil
that important function in their place. A resolution
of the Catholic Graduates Association brought the Uni-
vorsity Question before the General Council at its last

- zcéaona.

and capabilities. This is a problem which almost every

. Irishman desires to see settled, but which most English-
men are determined not to settle, it is one where there
is unanimity in principle combined with not a little
Jjealousy and dissension as to details, in fact eminently
a problem to be solved by an Irish representative body.
If the General Council desires to try its strength and

* capacity, there is no matter to which it can more profit-
ably turn its attention.

There is one defect which strikes one forcibly in the
recent proceedings of the Council. It is almost as much
addicted as a national convention to those hateful

_things, unanimous resolutions. When I sec a rcsol\}-
tion described as unanimous, I always think that it is

_really non-animous, that the truth is that nobody
really thinks or cares about the subject at all, or else
it is some impotent and meaningless compromise, which
tells us no more than that both parties are afraid to ex-
press their opinions. Dissension is the first fruit of
true representation and a representative body that only
passes unanimous resolutions, has ceased to be repre-
sentative at all. No doubt in a newly-created assembly
there will always be the fear that differences of opinion
may lead to its break up, but this is a danger it must
be prepared to incur.

The test of the General Council as a living institution
will be its capacity to deal with disputed questions and
to enforce its decisions in regard to them. If it can
establish a majority rule cheerfully acquiesced in, it
will have gone a long way to supply such a representa-
tive institution as we need. If on the contrary its
component atoms show a tendency to fly apart, if the de-
feated party are ever ready at any moment to exercise
their right to withdraw, the Council will either be
powerless to debate matters of really serious importance,
which I fear is more or less its present position, or else
it -will attempt to deal with them and pay the penalty
of dissolution. If this should be the case, as I hope it
will not, it will merely be seen that the construction of
this new institution was all along of too flimsy a descrip-
tion to permit of its bearing the strain of serious work.

One other difficulty, though not an insurmountable

one, seems to be ahead of the Council, that is its rela-
tion to the Irish party and the national political organi-
sation. Should a difference arise, which of them is to
be the authentic exponent of Irish opinion? As con-
trasted with the political organisation, the General
Council would evidently have a higher claim, but except
that it represents the opinion of Irish women as well as
Irish men, its claims would be only equal to those of
any party representing a clear majority of nationalist
voters. For since their secession Orange opinion must
in either case be left out of the question. If differences
however arise between the party and the Council party,
organisation will probably in the long run secure that
the same body of opinion shall be predominant in both.
We must at any rate all watch with interest the growth
and development of this new institution, remembering
that the British Parliament itself was once a body with
no greater powers than that of petitioning the King
for the redress of grievances. CHANEL.

IRISH PHONETICS.*

CARCELY less important than the preservation
S of the true idiom of the living Irish speech is
the preservation of the genuine sounds of the language.
Indeed, in some respects the preservation of the true
sound is more difficult and, in certain respects, more
important than the preservation of the true idiom. The
subtlest idioms can all be committed to paper, and pre-
served for the use of future generations; but at the
very best only an approximation to the sounds that are
peculiar to a” language and distinguish it from neigh-
bour tongues can be recorded or described.

Cicero or Virgil. And this in spite of the assistance
derivable from the metric of Latin poetry, in spite of
the circumstance that the Latin language has come
down to us in 4 or 5 transformed, but living, states,
that though crystalised into linguistic immortality, a
strong current of tradition concerning its sounds has
come down the ages, and that in one form or another
ts words and phrases have permeated every European
literature and language of the present generation. The
subtle and transient and over-varying nature of sound
hs.ls, up t_o'tlm present age, defied science to record it
with precision ; so much so that no living language, no
matter how pure its pedigree, no matter how little
adulterated it may have remained for centuries, can, in
its living state, supply the basis of an infallible recon-
struction of its ancient sounds.

Now to perpetuate a language in its living state, and
-at the same time to give its sounds a sudden wrench by
basing them, to a large extent, on the sounds of an-
other language, widely diverging from the first in its
sound system ; to do this is to produce a linguistic mon-
strosity which is certain to prove barren from the point
of view of literature, and whose existence as a living
speech must ever be precarious. Those, therefore, who
seek to perpetuate the living Irish language must en-
deavour to secure that not only must Irish be spoken
freely by the rising generation, but also that the sounds
of the Irish spoken be as far as possible identical with
the sounds of the language which is, alas! year by year
expiring on the lips of the adult generation of Irish
speakers in the remote sea boards and mountain fast-
‘nesses of the south and west,

It is, of course, only from correct native speakers
that the correct sounds of the language can be ob-
tained in all their purity, and sufficient time and inter-
course must be allowed before the learner can claim to
have grafted on his own speech-organs the sounds of
the living language as pronounced by his teacher. In
the case of children, so limber are their speech-organs,
so apt are they to imitate sounds, that, given due time
and a proper teacher, results of a most satisfactory
kind may be expected. —But teachers, whether native
speakers or not, will be greatly assisted by the use of a

_scientific system of phonetics.

There can be no doubt that the sounds of Irish pre-
sent many serious difficulties to learners whose organs
have been accustomed only to the production of Eng-
lish sounds. Still the Irish language as at present pro-
nounced, say, in Desmond, is a fairly consistent and
homogeneous whole, and lends itself to a fairly satis-
factory scientific treatment from a phonetic point of
view, even when the English language is the medium
in which such scientific treatment is conveyed. Closely
allied to the scientific treatment of the phonetics of
TIrish is the scientific treatment of the vexed question of
Irish orthography. The scientific investigation of
Irish phonetics is greatly hampered by the circumstance
that the so-called Irish alphabet, as it is at present
used, with its cloud of aspiration points, its eclipses, its
forest of silent and redundant letters on every page of
writing or print, can represent but very inadequately
and cumbrously the genuine sounds of the language.
The present writer has no patience with those who laud
the wonderful phonetic exactness and aptitude of the
Irish alphabet. The truth is that the Latin alphabet,
even in the time of its first adaptation to Irish, hung
round the sounds of that language like an ill-fitting gar-
ment. But with the lapse of centuries whole batches
‘of words have got shrunken in sound, or even quite
transformed, while this original ill-fitting orthographi-
cal garment now “ hangs round them like a lady’s loose
gown.”  We must, however, make the best of tho
materials at our disposal. It is not easy to coin a new

The little book just issued by the Christian Bro-
thers, “ Aids to the Pronunciation of Irish,”” which has
occasioned these remarks, is sound on the question of
Irish orthography. The teacher and advanced learner
will find in it useful hints as to the true pronunciation
of Irish letters and combinations of letters. — Though
it takes the Irish as spoken is Desmond as the basis of
investigation and analysis, it does not fail to present
every important variation from that standard to be
found in the other districts where Irish is still a living
speech. No doubt in teaching phonetics, oral instruc-
tion cannot be dispensed with, and for this reason the
course of instruction given by Dr. O’Daly at the Mun-
ster Training College during the session just terminated
must be of great utility to the students who attended
the College. But a book like the present will be of con-
siderable use in the class-room to refresh the teacher’s
memory on many points of phonetics.

It should be pointed out that in modern times the
phonograph may be used to advantage in teaching the
correct sounds of Irish. By the aid of the phonograph
not only can we obtain the correct sounds of vowels and
consonants and their combinations—allowance, of
course, being made for what may be termed phonetic
aberration—but the learner can also catch up the blas
in a sentence, song, or speech in such a way as to leave
a vivid impression of it on the memory.

I have not left myself space in this article to discuss
some of the many interesting points raised in this little
book, and to express my dissent, as in some cases T
should feel bound to do, from some of the statements
it contains, but, on the whole, the reader will find it
sound and solid in dealing with phonetics and ortho-
graphy.

A VISIT TO THE TOWER OF GLASS.

F ROM its earliest days the Leaper has done its

utmost in the cause of ecclesiastical art, espe-
cially urging that work should be given to Irish artists
who have, so far, been obliged to seek employment in
other countries, while Ireland was content to fill its
churches with stereotyped commonplace from Bavaria.
The recent exhibitions in Dublin show that a native art
is developing, and both at the Oireachtas and Horse
Show the stained glass this year has called forth espe-
cial praise.

Having admired it at the exhibitions I was led to
pay a visit to An Tup Zloine (the Tower of Glass), in
24 Upper Pembroke Street, where over two years ago
Miss Purser started her work with the object of keep-
ing young artists in Ireland. Her spirited enterprise
is attaining a well-merited success. The public are be-
ginning to appreciate the quality of the work turned
out, and the judges this year specially singled it out
commendation. At the National Competition,
South Kensington fully confirmed Miss Purser’s con-
tention that among Irish men and women real talent was
only waiting for the opportunity that has been lacking.
Miss Rhind was specially mentioned in their report—
a distinction gained by no other stained glass worker.

The aim of these works is to train the apprentices
and develop their individuality which is allowed full
scopo, though a high standard of good work and crafts-
manship is insisted on as the basis. Thus customers
have a variety in style and in painting from which to
choose what best accords with their own taste, and with
‘the Church for which their gift is designed. Instead
‘of the commercial window where the borders are filled
in with a monotonous pattern, you see at &n Tup
Sloine a conventional border, varied in each little sec-
tion at the pleasure of the designer, who has carried
out his work with delight, not as a mechanical task.

The originators of the French Academy aimed at the

control and modelling of the language of their country.

- They, however, were dealing with a living lan-

guage, whose growth and wvitality were too strong
for reconstruction and in effect ~they relinquished
the idea of controlling the stream which had
broadened into a majestic flood. The Gaelic League of
to-day has within its grasp the power for which the
Academy prayed—the power of modelling and perfecting
the language of which it is the custodian, the power of
rejecting the features that are useless, and of introduc-
ing the elements of life—and this great power is not
couscious{y used at all, while its wielders are uncon-
sciously rivetting upon the Irish speech the rusty fetters
of absurd orthography and impracticable paleeography.
The spelling and lettering of Irish “as she is printed "
are the forms that prevailed hundreds of years ago.
When the language was in its prime the forms were
hardly more characteristic of the Irish than of other
tongues. If the English language had become paralysed
for the same period, its written symbols would
now be as unintelligible to the eye of an Eng-
lishman accustomed to modern caligraphy and
spelling as is a page of Gaelic to a Cockney.
The patriotic Jingo who proposed that Englishmen
should emphasize their nationality by reverting to the
orthography and chirography of Chaucer, would be con-
signed to Bedlam. The Gaelic League has the choice
of two courses in the resurrection of the language. It
may give to the country the animated skeleton of a dead

- language with all the interest that attaches to anti-

quated printing and archaic spelling, or it may present
to the nation a living tongue equipped for modern use,
simple, legible, accurate.

classes where the teacher and pupils indulged in con-
versation I was encouraged by some hope of success,
but when I could attend class no longer, and had to rely
upon working at books in my own study I became hope-
loss. Tho chaotic mass of unaccustomed letters con-
cealed the words familiar to me. The simplest sounds
were disguised in the most complex forms. The thing
was disheartening. I am assured that when the stu-
dent has mastered tho rules (and the exceptions) he will
find reading Irish very easy. Surely if there is any
real desire to teach people Irish, it should be made
readable without any preliminary grinding in paleeo-
graphy or philology.” Of all our Gaelic scholars has no
ono ever heard of phonography or phonetics. Why will
the teachers not take up the spoken language as the
basis of teaching and re-produce the spoken language
phonetically in the ordinary Roman letters with the
few necessary modifications.  Why should the writing
of Gaelic be a slower or more cumbersome operation
than the writing of identical sounds in English? Does
it really assist the progress of the language movement
to address a letter in characters and spelling unfamiliar
to the immense majority of our people? On the other
hand is it not an encouragement to a student to demon-
strate that his task does not involve absolute innovation
in every branch of study?

Father O’Growney in the first book cites Mr. Pitman
as the great authority on phonetics. It is a pity that
the Gaelic League would not take up Pitman’s work and
apply it to the study of Irish. Persons familiar with
the training of children appreciate the marvellous ease
with which infants learn to read and write the Pitman
system of phonography. The system is universal in its
application. It is as applicable to Gaelic as to English.
It is simple, legible, accurate. Sir Isaac failed to
capture or regulate the English language for the same
cause that the Academy failed to control French. The
life of the language was too vigorous for restraint. The
League can as yet hardly complain that the life of the
movement is lusty beyond control. Is there any valid
reason then why the written reproduction of the spoken
language should not be modernised and rendered simple,
legible and accurate.

clusive invariable value. Sounds are thus reproduced

combination of symbols invariably represents the same
sound, which is represented by no other combination.

guage is written rationally and read easily. A much
